So I dug into this and it turns out that django overrides the default `email.message.Message:as_string` method. Now here's where it gets weird, looking at the default method vs the django one (in `django.core.mail.message.MIMEMixin:as_string`), aside from the django one doing some six.PY2/PY3 stuff and adding an as_bytes method (for py2/py3 cross functionality) the ONLY other difference* is how the Generator class is imported/used.

In the broken django version:
```
from email import generator
generator.Generator(...)
```

In the working default version:
```
from email.generator import Generator
Generator(...)
```

I tested monkeypatching the django as_string with the same logic except for different import and call and it works both for pants run and with a unexploded pex file.

Here's the monkeypatch
```
$ cat monkeypatches.py
def patched_as_string(self, unixfrom=False, linesep='\n'):
    """Return the entire formatted message as a string.
    Optional `unixfrom' when True, means include the Unix From_ envelope
    header.

    This overrides the default as_string() implementation to not mangle
    lines that begin with 'From '. See bug #13433 for details.
    """
    from email.generator import Generator # This is the patch!
    fp = six.StringIO()
    #g = generator.Generator(fp, mangle_from_=False) <- THIS FAILS
    g = Generator(fp, mangle_from_=False) # This is the patch!
    if six.PY2:
        g.flatten(self, unixfrom=unixfrom)
    else:
        g.flatten(self, unixfrom=unixfrom, linesep=linesep)
    return fp.getvalue()

def patch_django_core_mail_message_MIMEMixin():
    import django.core.mail.message
    django.core.mail.message.MIMEMixin.as_string = patched_as_string
    if six.PY2:
        django.core.mail.message.MIMEMixin.as_bytes = patched_as_string
```

And then in your settings.py

```
import monkeypatches
monkeypatches.patch_django_core_mail_message_MIMEMixin()
```

Clearly this is not a good long term solution, ymmv.

*I lied there's one other difference which is `Generator()` is called with `mangle_from_=False` but that doesn't seem to cause the problem.

It looks like we'll have to invoke the system `gcc -print-search-dirs`, then parse the output of that to find candidates for dirs containing `crti.o` (I already have code to do this, this interface is very stable). Since we're not using the system gcc to compile anything (so that we can e.g. support newer c++ features), we have to do this bootstrap step in order to create executables and to compile `python_dist()`s, because the location of the host-specific `crti.o` is partially baked into the gcc installation (as in, contains stuff that the distro `gcc` package maintainer added). We'll also need to add the `libc6-dev` package to the trusty images we use in CI (otherwise, they don't have `crti.o`). The native backend subsystem tests will ensure we can use clang to compile c/c++ as well using this -- the only reason we need the system `gcc` is to get the location of `crti.o`.

The above is all only for Linux hosts. We'll have a good error message like XCodeCLITools if `crti.o` could not be found. Implementing this now.

how are latencies calculated? it looks like a sum of latencies over last reporting window, which isn't useful. we should discuss correct reporting for these values.

if we report as individual data points (a list of latencies per CW call), then users can use standard CW aggregation to get useful stats like min/max/mean/med and percentiles.

if we want to report fewer datapoints, then we could collect our own summary stats, but then we'd need to choose some useful summary metrics, and report each of them separately. I prefer the separate data points approach. See https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/monitoring/PublishMetrics.html for some background

My testing shows that this `dtype` attribute issue only affects MLP and KNN model types. All other model types work correctly on Python 2.7 + Scikit-Learn 0.18.

> SEVERE: Failed to parse PKL net.razorvine.pickle.InvalidOpcodeException: invalid pickle opcode: 248

JPMML-SkLearn depends on the [Pyrolite](https://github.com/irmen/Pyrolite) library for low-level PKL file parsing functionality. Apparently, Pyrolite does not recognize Pickle protocol opcode `248` (hex `0xf8`).

I can't find the definition of this opcode in Python 3.6 codebase:
https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.6/Lib/pickle.py#L102-L179

> Could you give me pointers on where I might be looking to solve this error?

The stack trace of this exception points at method `net.razorvine.pickle.Unpikcler#dispatch(short)`. You could try addinng a `case` statement for opcode 248 there, and collect more information about it (eg. which Scikit-Learn class, which class attribute, etc.). Then, try to factor a minimal reproducible example, and open a parallel issue with the Pyrolite project: https://github.com/irmen/Pyrolite/issues

The JPMML-SkLearn project includes an integration test for the `IsolationForest` model type:
https://github.com/jpmml/jpmml-sklearn/blob/master/src/test/resources/pkl/IsolationForestHousingAnomaly.pkl

This PKL file was generated using Python 3.4(.3). So, downgrading from Python 3.6 to 3.4 may provide a temporary workaround.
Thank you!

This is a conversation we should migrate to a dev meeting or perhaps a google doc. However In summary:

For the Kolibri code base, I actually prefer that we _don’t_ squash and amend commits when changes are made based on code reviews, for two reasons:

* After a code review it's useful to see the specific changes which were made in response to feedback as individual commits. Squashing or amending loses that granular history.
* By default, PR branches [allow other project contributors to collaborate on them](https://help.github.com/articles/allowing-changes-to-a-pull-request-branch-created-from-a-fork/), meaning they are de facto 'shared' branches. For example in this case, @christianmemije may have decided to push changes directly to @mrpau-dev's branch rather than blocking the merge to speed up the process. Rewriting git history for shared branches makes things difficult for collaborators.

```python
adwords_client = adwords.AdWordsClient(..., cache=googleads.common.ZeepServiceProxy.NO_CACHE)
```

The reason behind this is that by default the library caches the SOAP WSDLs on your local filesystem. It will work just fine serverless if this is disabled.

@jeffbaumes when decorating a function and turning it into a task object,  what you've really done is given the function two purposes in life. The first purpose is to execute the body of the function. The second purpose is to act as a factory for producing messages. When you use the traditional method,  you skip the factory entirely and just put a message on the queue using ```app.send_message(...)```.  This means task specific configuration that effects the message behavior between when you call a factory method ```.delay(...)``` and when you put the message on the queue is lost (for example rate limiting the message production).  

What's more,  configurations that are passed directly to ```app.send_message(...)``` must be defined separately on the producer side (Girder) and the consumer side (Girder Worker).  This means task configurations are being stored in two separate locations, if they every go out of sync we will start seeing undefined, difficult to debug, behavior. That isn't an insurmountable problem,  but *it is a problem that Celery has already solved*. 

Synchronization is critical in this context (and just generally when dealing with distributed systems like this).  There are potentially other methods that could be used to synchronize task and application configurations (e.g. REST endpoints), but what Celery has chosen is the *least common denominator* for syncing across producer and consumer processes: an importable package.

Importable packages tightly bind code and configuration,  which at first glance may seem bad. In fact this is critical because the configuration is fundamentally about how your code will be executed. Maintaining those in the same place reduces complexity and makes synchronization errors less likely. An importable package is also a pre-requisite for at least the consumer side of the equation (so the task can actually execute its function body). Importable packages prevent having to run a specific service to manage configuration,  or sync configuration files across systems,  they provide a versioned environment that ensures consistent interpretations of configuration values. Importable packages have strong tooling around their installation and deployment (pip) and once they are installed they have 100% up time. 

Ultimately, the user need that is precipitating all these changes is the desire to leverage Celery's [canvas](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/userguide/canvas.html) features. This allows chaining, chunking, grouping and map-reducing tasks in a nice, intuitive syntax. The issue with using canvas features is *Girder is no longer the only producer*.  When one task is chained onto another,  the first task is produced inside girder,  it is executed and its return value is passed on as input to the next task.  The next task is produced from inside that Girder Worker process. WIth canvas features,  Consumers are also Producers!  If the application and the task configurations are different between Girder and Girder Worker this could have disastrous consequences,  where-by  the order of a tasks execution in its workflow effects the result of the individual task.

@akintolga 
1. On pa and ntb there were some ftp transmission that blocked for indefinitely time. Because of this the subscriber transmission worker is blocked for 30 minutes (1800s).
2. Because the expire lock is 5 minutes (300s), after 5 minutes another worker is blocked for 30 minutes for the same items

In order to avoid worker block for long periods of time I created a task for every transmission and I set a timeout for every transmission.
Also the task that acquire the lock has a timeout less than the lock expire so we will avoid the situation that multiple workers are used for the same items.

I think that the order of individual published items should not be so important because every item contain the publish time, and a client can use this info for ordering.

If this order is mandatory we have to serialize the transmission and we can set dependencies between celery task, one will be started only after the preview one is finished (use celery subtasks/chords).

Hi everyone,
Sorry about the delay.  I've been testing other wamp frameworks so that I can have more to report here.

Here's my take:  twisted and asyncio are still kind of at the fringes -- twisted is ancient and asyncio is still in the early phases of adoption.  I think that most people in the middle are using gevent.  And with good reason:  it has incredible library support and for the most part "it just works":   add `gevent.monkey.patch_all()` to your code and now it runs 10x faster (your mileage may vary).  

Gevent is explicitly supported by most of the database and messaging libraries that I've encountered. Here are a few that I'm using in my project:  
- [official mongo drivers](http://api.mongodb.com/python/current/examples/gevent.html)
- [official zmq bindings](http://pyzmq.readthedocs.io/en/latest/eventloop.html#pyzmq-and-gevent)
- [celery](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/_modules/celery/concurrency/gevent.html)
- [pypy](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/248)

On the other hand, I've found asycnio support to be much spottier and less mature, often coming from unofficial channels, and twisted support is nearly non-existent.  Even as asycnio builds momentum gevent is not going away, because the two libraries represent orthogonal (though not necessarily mutually exclusive) approaches -- implicit vs explicit -- and there will always be promoters in each camp.

So my user story is this:  I want autobahn to support gevent because:
- I have an existing code-base that I can't port to asyncio, *and/or* I need to use the wide array of mature libraries that support gevent
- **and** I want to interact with these services via WAMP, and autobahn and crossbar.io are the gold standard.  

WAMP services need to *do* something, or be *used* by something, and forcing users to adopt aysncio or twisted dramatically reduces the compatible choices.   

Here's a simple example: As a developer I'm very familiar with flask, and I have many existing flask applications.  Now from my flask app I want to use autobahn for WAMP.  But flask [doesn't seem to place nice with asyncio](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47841985/python3-asyncio-call-from-flask-route).  Do I work my way through these issues, switch to [aiohttp](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp), or [quart](https://gitlab.com/pgjones/quart), or something else?  Or do I decide it's too much effort and use a different tech?

Why not use another library, you ask?  I've tried all the other options and they're not good.  There are only 2, and I basically willed one of them into existence:

- [`gevent-websocket`](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/gevent-websocket/):  does not support WAMP v2.  its focus is mostly on general websocket compliance, and there are no tests for WAMP as far as I can tell. CI is not setup.  little maintenance.  
- [`wampy`](https://github.com/noisyboiler/wampy): I had to make a [PR](https://github.com/noisyboiler/wampy/pull/61) to port this from eventlet to gevent.  Also doesn't support WAMP v2.  I've started testing it and it's quite buggy even for very basic RPC and PUB/SUB.  It has a travis CI setup and a decent test suite (tests against crossbar) but evidently the coverage is poor.

Let's face it, crossbar.io is driving the development of WAMP, and other libraries lag behind in just about every way: feature adoption, crossbar compatibility, testing.  I have very little confidence in the stability of these projects or in the outlook for their long term maintenance.

So I'm currently faced with some tough choices:
1. port all of my code to asyncio:  not going to happen
2. use wampy everywhere that I need gevent and deal with updating wampy to WAMP v2, fixing bugs, and keeping it up to par with autobahn
3. make autobahn work with gevent

I'm afraid that option 2 will be the same amount of work as option 3, just spread out over a longer period of time.  

> Autobahn is 100% written in an event-based style and I'm not sure how/if this is compatible with gevent?  

The tests here shows that it's quite possible to build something in an event-based style using gevent.  What they don't show is whether this will scale.

> Do you have a story about how this support would work in the end? While txaio helps, the asyncio and Twisted support in Autobahn comes from a bunch of framework-specific code. How, for example, would something like "autobahn.twisted.component.py" be written to use gevent?

The naive approach would be to introduce `autobahn.gevent`, but looking through `autobahn.asyncio`, it feels like *most* of this code could be reused for gevent.  The number of direct calls to `asyncio` are minimal, so  it might even be possible make a mock `asyncio` library using `gevent` under the hood, that covers the remaining uses.  That would reduce the amount of specialized code in autobahn that needs to be maintained. 

> In short: merging the PR would in a way imply that we aim for full gevent support in Autobahn, and I just don't feel ready for that;)

I completely understand.   There's little point in merging this until there are concrete plans to support gevent in autobahn.  If you're not *opposed* to gevent support, then I might take a deeper look at what it would take on the autobahn side, to see if I can put together a working proof of concept.  If your team would be unwilling to support gevent even with this work completed, then please let me know so that I can focus my efforts elsewhere.

Sorry for this epic post.  I hope you made it here!  ;)

Rough sketch for refactoring the fast sync process (which I don't think needs to happen in Grace Hopper):

### Header Sync
- roughly keep header sync the same (use our best peer exclusively, until we catch up to them), but...
- dump the headers into a priority queue (prioritized by low block number), and immediately request the next set
- if the queue gets too full (maybe `>= 2* MAX_REQUEST_HEADERS`), stop requesting headers
- if the queue gets empty enough (maybe `<= MAX_REQUEST_HEADERS`), start requesting headers again

### Block body sync
- keep track of which peers have a request in flight, and which are available
- loop through all available peers and make requests for any headers that have been downloaded but not processed
- when receiving block parts, re-add headers to the queue, for block parts that were not received
- can probably stop doing the speed-weighted requests to peers from #1009 

### Block persistence
- in block number order, persist a block once we have its header, body, and receipt, pausing if we are missing one of those pieces (and handling potential forks)

_Note to self: where are we persisting receipts?_

I had to refresh my memory because it's been a while since this code was written, but it turns out we are already performing that check when we receive a pong. This is how it works:
1. When we [send a ping, we get the msg hash](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/p2p/discovery.py#L190-L191) and [generate a pingid, combining the hash with the node's ID](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/p2p/kademlia.py#L470)
2. That pingid is then used to track [pongs we are waiting for](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/p2p/kademlia.py#L484)
3. [If we get a pong with a hash different than what we expect, we drop it](https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/p2p/kademlia.py#L342-L349)

Yeah, this makes sense. I like that we are trying to be loud and really making sure people understand that things are deprecated and changing soon, but this mechanism takes away too many options from the end user.

Let's:

1. Remove all the filter lines here (lines 17 & 25) and
2. Add configuration to make sure that we display deprecation warnings during our pytest runs, because we *do* want to see deprecation warnings when running our own tests.

Unfortunately, deprecation warnings are not displayed by default, last time I checked, hence this hack to be loud about it. It makes me a little nervous that people won't be informed, by default, about upcoming deprecations. But overriding warning filters is not the right way to solve the problem. A better solution doesn't come to mind, so we should just remove the hack.

is indeed invalid, because `==` means an *exact* version match, which isn't compatible with a wildcard or range.

    conda install "numpy 1.8.*"

is fine as of conda 4.4.

I introduced 3 variables pertaining to activate/deactivate. In the below examples I show how to use one of the variables (`CONDA_ENVNAME`). The others are `CONDA_HELP` and `CONDA_VERBOSE`, both of which are boolean (true/false) values. All of this is explained in the expanded help dialog for activate and deactivate (see `. activate -h` and `. deactivate -h`). @msarahan mentioned adding aliases to `source activate` to users' .profile at the same time as adding conda bin to the PATH. As far as I understand that is not something that is implemented in conda but rather in the actual Anaconda installation. Implementing such a change would mean that changes should also be made to conda/install.py/print_activate to detect whether or not an alias exists in the current shell and what shell is running. I also had a similar problem(also referring to #6861 ) with my conda environment.
I went through both the issue discussion and this is my conclusion(put in very simple words):
Whenever inside a conda environment, if you need a package to be installed avoid using pip and use conda. pip will mess with the way conda organizes the environment(s). So do a conda install instead of a pip installed.
Please let me know if that is correct
I ask this because this has been my question every since I've started using conda: the correct way of installing packages inside a conda environment. I've tried searching for a solution and on most of the links I've read that both the ways are correct; that was until I ran into this issue. Some sort of conclusion will be really helpful. Thanks!

It might be useful to write a set of autoconf macros to generate and substitute macros whose values are paths to `XYZDIR` relative to `ABCDIR`, then programs can use those, and then there would be no need for things like the `relocate()` function provided by the GNU `relocatable-prog` module.  Also, programs generally need to be able to run in place, in the build tree, for testing purposes, so additional macros may be needed, and programs may need to try two paths to reach installed artifacts, or else the built tree may need to be re-organized.

Clearly it will be hard to boil the oceans, but at least stopping the rot should be valuable.

I don't know the history of data types in SAM, but it looks like they are just trying to be backward compatible with some older version of SAM.

> Will make sure the missing formats are added.

Thank you.

Would it be possible to change `d` to `f` in the automatic type detection step? Adding a new tag with a float value to an SAM existing record currently makes the record invalid according to the current specification.

This might not be trivial: you probably want to keep existing `d`'s as `d`'s, but SAM types are deduced from Python data types, and Python implements only doubles (but they are called floats). I see 3 possible solutions:
* storing SAM datatype as an integer/string (like in you [example](https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/develop/sam.c#L1070), unfortunately Python doesn't have char)
* an internal bit flag for numbers (double vs. float)
* [array](https://docs.python.org/3/library/array.html) with a single entry (then it's possible to distinguish between doubles and floats)

Thanks. Pysam is following the format definitions implemented in htslib, see here:

https://github.com/samtools/htslib/blob/develop/sam.c#L1070

Will make sure the missing formats are added.

For alembic seeing the rowId, I think there should be a big yellow message somewhere for anyone who might stumble across this. Often, you can use `alembic revision --autogenerate` and it will compare its migration HEAD with the database state. Seeing this discrepancy would cause some issues and the only workaround is for someone to add the rowId to their models. If it does not exist in the models, each autogenerated migration will attempt to drop the column.

I'm sorry you had a hard time navigating the documentation, but [the alias action](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/alias.html) is clearly at the top of the list of [actions](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/actions.html) available in Curator.

To create an alias, you only need to [name](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/option_name.html) it, and then use the [add](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/alias.html) filter block to select which indices you want associated with the [name](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/option_name.html) you selected.  You can even apply [extra settings](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/5.4/option_extra_settings.html#_link_linkend_alias_alias_link) to your alias.

1. `get_required_fees` doesn't work correctly for Transfer_from_blind and Blind_transfer operations in bitshares-core. Instead a local fee calculator is used. And yes `5 * GRAPHENE_PRECISION` is hard-coded, on the wallet side!

2. When doing Transfer_from_blind, you have to include the fee INTO Blind_transfer amount, meaning you have to pre-calculate the fee for the operation, before it is fully constructed. This is not a serious issue, as long you only have to do it once, however...

3. ...there is yet another bug/issue in bitshares-core, which forces you to precalculate this fee TWO times, for one and for two outputs, so we can actually avoid TWO round-trips to server.

Plus: I think it's nice to have such a feature. You cache fee definitions, and then you can re-calculate fees locally, without querying the server.

Just a couple of examples about how the developer could use the new request tracing feature. I prefer a top to bottom implementation which helps me to make sure that we will meet all of the requirements that we are exposing here.

From my point of view the main requirement that we should meet are:

* A request tracking system that links all of the signals that were triggered by the same request for a specific ClientSession instance.
* The scope of the context == Request scope
* Follow the same Signals exposure of `web.Application` class
  
The first example [1] shows how these requirements are exposed to the user:

* Signals are finally attached to the `ClientSession` class, there is no `RequestTrace` object.
* Enable or disable means attach or not attach listeners.
* `trace_context` is related to each request, therefore its instantiated at each _post_, _get_, _whatever_ method.
* Developer can give an alternative `trace_context` instance as a new keyword for the post, get, whatever method.

The second example [2] is a more complicated one which subclasses the `ClientSession`, having, as a result, a new class that allows the user to store some variables that won't mutate for all of the requests using a specific class instance.

Leaving aside the singals/interceptors supported, and the signature of these methods I believe that this way to expose the request tracing feature might be a good candidate IMHO to build the final implementation.

An alternative, intermediate solution option to a complex UI could be a kernel-side filter that simply looks at all display messages and trims any plaintext ones above a (configurable) threshold by snipping their middle section out ("hollowing them out"). If the default threshold is high enough, it will cover the immense majority of cases and I doubt anyone will ever notice. 

And the rare person who really needs to display 1,000,000 lines of text in their notebooks for some bizarre reason, can always bump up the threshold.

This could be prototyped purely on the IPython kernel by hooking directly into its execution engine, and if it works well, it could be put on the server itself as a filter that applies to all kernels before data is sent out to the clients.

This would alleviate the need for lots of UI design on this topic.

[Xref #359]

The docstring of `export_loop` states:

```
:param field_converter:
    This function is applied to every field found in ``instance_or_dict``.
```

But in actuality there's this:

```python
if hasattr(field, 'export_loop'):
    shaped = field.export_loop(...)
else:
    shaped = field_converter(...)
```

Thus, putting a model through `export_loop` always produces a `dict`, regardless of what the supplied field converter was.

What this means is that there are two notions of "native" when it comes to models:

  * When importing, `to_native` generates model instances.
  * When exporting, `to_native` generates dictionaries.

The point, as I understand it, is that you can run `Model.to_native()` and send the result to, say, PyMongo, which understands various Python types but not models.

Now, imagine a custom type `FooType` that takes some input and converts it to an instance of `FooClass`. Running `Model.to_native()` would preserve the `FooClass` representation, rendering the result useless to PyMongo.

So, clearly it's not enough to special case models. Each type should be able to decide for itself whether the "interoperable" representation should be the native or the primitive form (or perhaps something else entirely).

To put it another way, two conversion methods are not enough. There should be three:

  * `to_native` to create the internal, rich representation (`datetime`, `Model`, `FooClass`)
  * `to_python` to create the interoperable representation (`datetime`, `dict`, `dict`)
  * `to_primitive` to create the language-independent representation (`str`, `dict`, `dict`)

Virtually all types could just map `to_python` to either `to_native` or `to_primitive` as appropriate.

`Model.to_python()` would be equivalent to the `Model.to_native()` of today, except that it would be able to correctly deal with all rich constructs, not just models.

***

`export_loop` was meant to be independent of the desired output format, just blindly applying the field converter. This plan would take that design to its conclusion. Of course there are many ways to accomplish the same, so I'd like to hear what others think.

Since we are simply comparing string, I think the form with `STREQUAL` is more appropriate.

A recent commit from CMake core dev also seems to indicate using `STREQUAL` is sufficient. See https://github.com/Kitware/CMake/commit/be73273952974e15ed12cf41180162bbbda70da3

Also worth noting the `STREQUAL` form is what is used in the CMake code base to protect simple string comparison, I thought to make things consistent. See below.

All of that said, if using `MATCHES`  protects from issues not covered by `STREQUAL`, we should look into also updating CMake code base.

```
cd /path/to/source/cmake
$ ack --cmake "x\\$\{.+STREQUAL" -l | wc -l
67
$ ack --cmake "x\\$\{.+MATCHES" -l | wc -l
13

```

I like the idea of a longer than one year registration. Somewhere in the range of 2-5 years seems ideal. The 10-20 range seems far too long, and infinite is just a bad idea all around.  

It may be best to somewhat follow domain registrations where you can register initially for 2 years or so, and then have the option to extend it up to something like 5 years for an additional fee. 

People forgetting to renew will always be an issue, and having a "redemption period" like domains do and  @csuwildcat noted would help with that. When the id expires, any client should treat any data for that id as invalid and notify the client. That way they are notified something is wrong if they are still using the id. However, the id will not be available to be registered by someone new for 6 months or so during which it can still be renewed by the original owner.

There could also be services in the future that could help by checking name expiration and reminding users via email to renew before the id expires.

Just reporting back that we have a successful upload process working. Here is our `buildspec.yml` commands that correspond to the CodeCov process.

```yaml
version: 0.2

phases:
  build:
    commands:
      - ...

  post_build:
    commands:
      - VCS_PULL_REQUEST=$(echo $CODEBUILD_SOURCE_VERSION | sed 's/pr\///g')
      - COV_SCRIPT=/root/.cache/codecov.sh
      - if [ ! -f "$COV_SCRIPT" ]; then curl -s https://codecov.io/bash > "$COV_SCRIPT"; fi
      - echo "$CODEBUILD_INITIATOR" | grep GitHub && bash "$COV_SCRIPT" -t $CODECOV_TOKEN || true

cache:
  paths:
    - '/root/.cache/codecov.sh'
```

As a summary here are the things I've changed/added:

- async/await aren't keyword in python despite the common intuition, those are "NAME", so I've changed them to that (this required some tweaks in the grammar parsor and quite a lot of exploration)
- there were quite some bug like missing definitions in render.py, a function name overwrite, a additional declaration for funcdef that was unused, async where added in "for a in b: pass\nelse: pass" case
- design modification: await is not an alone node but a node with a value which is the wrapper atomtrailers (I'm still not 100% sure that this is the most intuitive way)
- async is not an alone node anymore but attributes to the wrapper nodes to flatter the things
- add way more tests

I'm not 100% sure everything is fine and will hold on the future, the work on RedBaron (when I'll do it...) should confirm that or raises new bugs.

I agree with @rplevka here and this is what I have been advocating for a long time now.  We should just follow the dev model here:

* Most of the time, we do not spend time fixing automation code for an older Satellite version and focus only on the master - The issue which @rplevka just discovered is just an example of this :) 
* It is okay to support only the latest Satellite version in nailgun master.  If we really need to backport something very important to an older version, we need to cherrypick, which I don't see us doing much anyways.  There is no point in continuing to write ugly code like this (see below) anymore.  Simplicity is the way to go - together with proper branching as explained in the document linked below.

```
if version == 6.1 `do only this`
  else if version == 6.2 or 6.2.4 `do just this`
  else `error out`
```

Also:
* if pulp smash likes to communicate to older satellite version, it can use a specific branch to do so.  This should not prevent us in continuing to maintain nailgun master for all different versions - it is just too much overhead and the overhead will continue to increase in the future.
* More information about branching process here: https://url.corp.redhat.com/b60264b

Hello,

You are correct.

As per documentation, sshuttle does not support intercepting IPv6 traffic unless running on Linux using the TPROXY method.

https://github.com/sshuttle/sshuttle/blob/master/README.rst

This is due to limitations in the underlying firewall implementations. e.g. on Linux the REDIRECT target is IPv4 only. (At least this was the case when I last researched this issue)

If you are using Linux you may be able to switch to using the TPROXY method (see above documentation), otherwise you could be out of luck.

Regards

Typical ETL but there will be periods of many hours we don't expect new data so need to prevent these timeouts killing the programs.

librdkafka (the underlying C client that powers the python client) will use the system resolver to solve the broker hostname. On some systems, OSX in particular, the `localhost` entry in `/etc/hosts` resolves both to an IPv4 and IPv6 address, so librdkafka will in a round-robin fashion attempt to connect to all addresses the hostname resolves to.

To limit what address families it tries to connect to you can set the `broker.address.family` configuration property to `v4`.

I've been looking at this issue over the past few days. From what I've read in the RFC, I see nothing the prohibits adjusting the time zone for an automatically generated DTSTART to match that of UNTIL when DTSTART is not explicitly specified.

I believe I have come up with a working change to the implementation, but am running into an interesting challenge when writing a test case. Ideally, the test case should compare the behavior of a rule when DTSTART is explicitly specified to the results of another rrule where DTSTART is not specified. Writing a test case like that would involve DTSTARTs being generated using datetime.now([tz]) at two different times, in which case there may enough of a difference between the two DTSTARTs to cause the rrules to have different results. Using rrules with larger frequency would minimize the chance of the problem occurring, but not completely eliminate it. Maybe I am overthinking the problem, but I figured that I would bring it up before going any further.

There is of course the option of writing a test case that is less thorough but does not have the potential to fail in that way.

The problem with your example is that you're not using `many=True` correctly. Most field types do not accept `many` option (at least the way you think). For field types other than Nested, `many` argument is treated as a *metadata*, some information that is stored in field type (that's why you do not get "unknown argument" errors), and as far as I can tell that metadata is not used in any way by the library itself.
In order to create a proper list of integers, you need to do
```python
class S(Schema):
    nums = fields.List(fields.Integer())
```
That way you could encode default/missing values there:
```python
class S(Schema):
    nums = fields.List(fields.Integer(), missing=[1, 2, 3])
```
The reason why `default` argument works for you is that when dumping it just takes whatever you put there and put into output, but for loading it actually tries to parse it (although you still need to supply it as a list of integers, not a JSON string of list of integers).

```python
class S(Schema):
    nums = fields.List(fields.Integer(), default=[4, 5, 6], missing=[1, 2, 3])


print S().dump({})
# => MarshalResult(data={u'nums': [4, 5, 6]}, errors={})
print S().load({})
# => UnmarshalResult(data={'nums': [1, 2, 3]}, errors={})
```

Just a quick check if these API updates are approved before I push them to GitHub:

Normal file connections have a read-only closed Bool variable that the user can read to check if the file is closed. Added this to LoomConnection too. Basically, self._closed is set to false by __init__, and to true by close(), and accessible through a closed getter:

        @property
        def closed(self) -> Bool:
                return self._closed

Currently, calling ds.close() on an already closed connection raises an error because it calls self._file.close() after setting self._file to none.

I'm somewhat on the fence on this one, since throwing an error could arguably be considered the right behaviour (telling the programmer to not call close() superfluously).

OTOH, trying to close an already closed connection is harmless, and very annoying if it interrupts a long-running script. Also, calling f.close() twice on a regular file does nothing, so that's apparently the Pythonic way that users will expect.

The lack of feedback is a bit stupid IMO, so as a compromise I added a check + a warning for now:

        def close(self) -> None:
                """
                Close the connection. After this, the connection object becomes invalid.
                """
                if self._file is None:
                        # Warn user that they're being paranoid and should clean up their code
                        logging.warn("Connection to %s is already closed", self.filename)
                else:
                        self._file.close()
                        self._file = None
                self.row_attrs = {}
                self.col_attrs = {}
                self.shape = [0, 0]

I am having second thoughts about this:
May be we do not have a always an exception that refers to all the licenses it "excepts" to?
In which case tracking a list of which main licenses an exception is for would be a moot point entirely and a waste of time and energy...
And any validation of an expression would become moot as there would be case where the expression would be considered invalid but would still be valid otherwise.

So I digged a bit what are the ranges of exceptions out there... I reviewed ALL the exceptions we have at SPDX.org and in ScanCode.
The vast majority apply to either LGPL or GPL various versions and various "or later" or not.
Only a few apply to the CPL and Apache license or even one to the BSD.
So I am thinking that tracking and validating which license an exception is for may be overkill.

If this is the case this would simplify much the implementation as we would NOT/could NOT validate the license on the left side of an `XXX with YYY` expression  .... e.g. it could be any value (with some crazy possibilities, though)

Even just collecting which exact list of licenses an  exception is for is NOT a trivial thing. To be accurate and comprehensive the complexity is rather high. So all in all I am in favor to eschew that complexity entirely, eg. an exception would only be tagged by a flag for now 

we could always add validation against a list later if needed.... but removing is later would be tough
So we would allow idiotic `MIT with GCC-3.1 exception` but that is not a big deal

I also recall the SPDX group passed that too because of its complexity
And FWIW, while writing the validation code in license_expression, this was starting to smell really bad too :stuck_out_tongue:

So no tracking which license an exception may apply to (and therefore without validating if a license+exception combo makes sense or not)
And what I mean byt not validating I mean this:

- `GPL-2.0 with Classpath-exception` is valid because `GPL-2.0` is a license and `Classpath-exception` is tagged as an exception
- `Classpath-exception with GPL-2.0` is invalid because GPL-2.0 is not an exception
- `MIT with Classpath-exception` is valid because we will not for now track which license(s)  the `Classpath-exception` may apply to (edited)

So the last case is the one we will not do validation for now and may never as it may not be practically possible to do correctly in some cases

We recently made a simple field for our code to make it work with django-polymorphic. I'm a big fan of a more explicit approach with this field than doing lots of magic in `ResourceRelatedField`. 

The fundamental reason for this to work is that we need to get the model name based on the object ID with the queryset. That will make the type verification work.

```Python
from rest_framework_json_api.exceptions import Conflict
from rest_framework_json_api.relations import ResourceRelatedField
from rest_framework_json_api.utils import get_resource_type_from_instance


class PolymorphicRelatedField(ResourceRelatedField):
    """Polymorphic Related Field.

    A field to ignore failed type verification in
    `ResourceRelatedField.to_internal_value`. Rest Framework JSON API does not
    support Django-polymorphic yet. Its type verification is based on
    retrieving resource name from `queryset.model` on the field. In our case
    that will always return the base model name, which will fail for sub models.
    """

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        """Override to ignore `Conflict` exception.

        Since the resource name is retrieved from `queryset.model` on the
        field, we make sure it's ignored.
        """

        try:
            return super().to_internal_value(data)

        except Conflict:
            obj = self.queryset.filter(id=data['id']).first()
            if data['type'] != get_resource_type_from_instance(obj):
                raise

            # Luckily the Conflict exception is raised at the end so we can
            # explicitly call super on `ResourceRelatedField`, which is the
            # `PrimaryKeyRelatedField`.
            return super(ResourceRelatedField, self).to_internal_value(
                data['id'])
```

`to_representation()` will just work out of the box because django-polymorphic returns the correct sub type of the model. What do you think?

I don't think this is something that should be implemented within `django-import-export` directly.  It currently doesn't handle any of the other admin permissions like that, and actually the way I use it in 2.1 I intentionally want users who only have the new `View` permissions to be able to import (I want them to be able to import but not change once imported).

Especially since this functionality is so new, I think the appropriate way to do this would be to implement `ExportMixin.has_export_permission()` and `ImportMixin.has_import_permission()` in your admin classes to check that permission.

Or maybe the default implementations of both of those would take into account `View`/`Change`/`Delete`/etc.  But that gets a little complicated and would be backwards incompatible.

If your project requires certain versions of Django (or any other package), it should specify those requirements in `requirements.txt` or `setup.py` or however you install dependencies.

`django-import-export` does `import` from Django packages, do Django is a _requirement_ for `django-import-export` to function, so Django **should** be listed in the requirements for `django-import-export`.  That may have other consequences when using `pip` to install packages manually, but there are ways to mitigate those.

The CFG that angr produces is an _interprocedural_ CFG, which tracks control flow across an entire program, not just in a single function. As a result, the successor of a basic block ending in a call is the target of the call.

However! angr also generates what are called "fake return" successors, which go from a call to its return target. These are hidden from you by default, but you can see them with `cfg.get_successors(node, excluding_fakeret=False)`. Alternately, the individual function objects in `cfg.functions` have a `.graph` property that should be a networkx graph describing the intraprocedural (the opposite of interprocedural) CFG.

Something we should also consider is that this issue has applications for normal analysis as well - if there's a block, including delay slot instruction, that is exactly 100 instructions long, VEX will cut it off at 99 instructions, ending with the jump instruction but missing the jump slot, and cause this issue.

Really, VEX was designed with the intent in mind of being a sequential translator, and one of our patches was to make the delay slot static data NOT persist over translations because that was causing serious issues with some of our uses of VEX, e.g. for patching single instructions.

What we can do is to check if a vex block for MIPS ends with a jump instruction but not its delay slot, and manually cut off the jump instruction if it does. This'll force the issue for the single-instruction case, by returning an empty block and halting execution, and fix the 100-instruction-block case by making the jump show up in the next block entirely.

And of course we can complain loudly if we try to single-step mips code. Should the complaint be a logging message (warning or error?) or an exception?

Wow this was tough to get working, but I think it functionally works as expected. I've refactored composite config loading and the dependency tree used to read/load datasets. Important changes made so far:

 - Compositor objects are loaded and instantiated during `Scene.__init__`. This made dependency tree creation much easier to separate from the Scene. Previously these were loaded *iff* a dataset couldn't be found in the loaded readers
 - Multiple calls to `Scene.load` should work properly
 - Calls to `Scene.load` in a resampled Scene _should_ work if all dependencies needed from readers already exist, but it hasn't really been tested.
 - `Scene.available/all_composite_ids/names` methods only return non-modifier composites

Answers to original questions after talking with @mraspaud:

1. We would prefer to have the fully qualified DatasetIDs (b). Currently this PR only does the simple DatasetID (a). I don't think it is possible to do (b) reliably.
2. It would be nice to know the composite DatasetID before, but given the flexibility of a compositor I don't think this is possible. Currently this PR only handles the DatasetID on config read.
3. Yes, there are elements for each element in the DataseID.

This can also be simplified, as converting to a time block and back after adding 1 is redundant and simpler to calculate as an offset to `for_time` below.

Here is my implementation in Lua
The functionality for valid_window can be removed by making it 0 (for checking single key bound to valid_window = 1 iterations lifetime).
```
totp.valid_until = function(instance, for_time, valid_window)
	valid_window = valid_window or 0 -- ensure number is at least 0
	return for_time + ((self.interval + 1) * valid_window) -- do time offset calculation
end
```

> I thought gameIds and matchIds were the same.

You may be right. I know back in the old API, there was a `/games` endpoint and a `/matches` endpoint which had different ids and I assumed that since the gameId I was getting not working that was still true-ish. But I did not wait until the game was over, so that's probably what it was. 

If that's the case, then we can do something like this:

* First Run
    * Load the current list of patches.
    * Grab a match from the featured matches end point.
* Subsequent Runs
    * Grab the match data for the remembered match. Compare its patch to the last known patch. If it is different, add the new patch with the start time of the match.
    * Grab a new match from the featured matches end point.

The only thing I haven't thought of here is how to deal with downtime (for example, if the program stops running and a patch is deployed while it is running).

> Also, patches should be set per-region. Right now they are global, but that's not actually correct.

Yeah. I meant to mention that in the OP. This logic will need to run for each region. We'll probably have to change the structure of the patches.json file (or else have multiple) to account for multiple regions.

Something like:
```json

  {
    "season": "Season 7",

        "EUW": 1502251200.0,
        ...
    },

        "NA": 1503460800.0,
        "EUW": 1503460800.0,
        ...
    },
  },
...
```

> Another issue involved here is getting the updated patches to users.

Yeah. You can just have it be pulled from github or some other CDN. The updated version should always be available at this address: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/meraki-analytics/cassiopeia/master/cassiopeia/patches.json. 

You can have it download it whenever the master list is grabbed and then have it fall back to the local file if for some reason the master list is not available.

Using `if match is None` is the correct way to test if the match was pulled successfully. We deliberately do not support `if match == None` because python is moving away from supporting this entirely, the `is` keyword is more correct for cases like this.

Doing things like `if ___ is None` can be done throughout Cassiopeia for various objects/attributes, including `match.participants[0].stats.node_capture_assists` as another example that accesses a specific attribute of a match rather than the entire match itself. Riot may not return data for every object/attribute, so we set any unreturned values to None.

>And then I found the region of this match is 'EUW', which means the region setting is useless?

If you are making the call `https://na.api.pvp.net/api/lol/na/v2.2/match/2405929288?includeTimeline=0&api_key=XXX` and it is returning an `euw` match, there is nothing we can do about that. The correct call to `na` is being made, Riot is just returning what may be the wrong match. Without seeing more of your output and code I cannot be sure.

>I reconsidered this problem, and found that if Cass. returned 404 correctly, there should not be this problem. So I think the exception part of Cass. should be checked.

Yes, 404s are handled in Cass by setting the object that is returned to `None`. This is intended. Are you proposing to raise an exception on a 404 instead?

I would prefer we take the approach in #1075.

1. This change causes Beholder dashboard to make assumptions about parent DOM, thereby limiting its modularity (e.g. embed *just* Beholder w/o TB in Jupyter). #1075 solves this via standard Polymer attached / detached events.

2. setTimeout() is a suboptimal workaround for `tf_backend.RequestManager` lacking a veneer for `XMLHttpRequest::timeout`. While RequestManager is a cool veneer, Beholder's XHR intensity has illuminated why we can't be cool anymore. The removal of RM shouldn't block this work.

My opinion is that DOM elements ought to be removable. This requires that they store state information appropriately. We don't have time to make that transition for all plugins. However we can do that for Beholder today.

1. I agree this isn't the ideal long-term state, but I would prefer to as a rule to avoid cherry-picking changes to tf-tensorboard onto a release branch if we don't have to.  I can send a follow-up post-release that changes it to have tf-tensorboard set an explicit selected property on the dashboard element, which would be compatible with embedding Beholder and avoid an assumption about parent DOM.

2. Yes, as mentioned in the PR description I agree that native XHR timeout handling would be better, but I would prefer to revisit that holistically across plugins rather than making one-off changes that result in Beholder diverging from how requests are handled by all the other dashboards.  The issue with timeouts isn't specific to Beholder; it affects all the plugins pretty much equally.  It merits a general solution.

I don't think removing the whole dashboard DOM is the right solution to this problem.  It's not true of web apps in general, and certainly not of other plugin dashboards, that the state in the DOM is reflected durably outside the DOM.  There are plenty of client-side controls in normal web apps that have a natural lifetime that matches their DOM elements.  If we were to change that for TensorBoard, it would mean either dashboards would no longer remain the way the user left them when navigating away and back, or they would remain with their last-used settings even across launches of TensorBoard, both of which are major departures from the tried-and-true behavior of the current dashboards.

Beyond that, as I mentioned, DOM removal of the entire dashboard is a blunt tool.  It doesn't lend itself to several advantages I mentioned in my PR description, like the fact that this PR goes further by disabling XHRs when the video has been paused, which we can't achieve with the DOM removal approach because we still need to be rendering the entire dashboard.  This PR's approach ensures that Beholder doesn't send a continuous stream of network requests unless it's actually displaying a continuous stream of new data, whereas with #1075 we still send a lot of unnecessary traffic.

IEEE 802.15.4-2006 has the following definition for "coordinator":

> 3.9 coordinator: A full-function device (FFD) capable of relaying messages. If a coordinator is the principal controller of a personal area network (PAN), it is called the PAN coordinator.

At the very least, all Thread Routers should be considered IEEE 802.15.4 coordinators.  Whether or not REEDs are also considered IEEE 802.15.4 coordinators depends on the interpretation of "capable".

The primary purpose of transmitting beacons is to announce the presence of a network.  If REEDs and rx-on-when-idle end devices do not transmit beacons, then it is theoretically possible for a neighboring networks to use the same PAN ID.  Unfortunately, the way IEEE 802.15.4 ACKs work, two networks using the same PAN ID could result in ambiguous acknowledgements.

When you are using the mean square error, it is already computing a distance between X and Y. If you want to add a new metric, you can add a custom class based on this template: https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/tflearn/metrics.py#L128

Basically, predictions are predicted values f(X), targets is Y and inputs is X. You can build any custom new metric and pass it as argument of `regression` layer.
In your case, should be something like:
```
class EuclidianDistance(Metric):

    def __init__(self, name=None):
        super(EuclidianDistance, self).__init__(name)
        self.name = "EuclidianDistance" if not name else name

    def build(self, predictions, targets, inputs):
        """ Build euclidian distance tensor. """
        self.built = True
        self.tensor = YOUR_DISTANCE_OP(predictions, targets, inputs)
        # Add a special name to that tensor, to be used by monitors
        self.tensor.m_name = self.name
```

`YOUR_DISTANCE_OP` is the custom operation that will compute the score.

@SilverGuo The issue is originated in the UTF-8 encoding, there is:
- `U+0020 SPACE`
- `U+202F NARROW NO-BREAK SPACE`

Obviously, during training the latter one is treated as part of the word while the former is a proper token seperator. Long story short: You will always get the same vector, when you query for 'the'.
Just in the rare case, where you query for `<U+202F NARROW NO-BREAK SPACE>the`, you would give the different ones. So there are **no** multiple vectors for the same term, but the difference in the terms is at some point not visible (you will typically not notice a narrow non-breaking space instead of a usual space). 

For`the` example: I had a quick look: the following word vectors are provided:

- `the`
- `#the`
- `the%`

Some notes comparing those two, `da.random.uniform` (good) and zarr (bad).

Identical up on the initial pass through `stack` up until the items
`(da.random.uniform, 0, 0)` / `(X.zarr, 0, 0)`. They have different dependents.

- random uniform has no deps
- `(X.zarr, 0, 0)` has one: `{'zarr-X.zarr'}`

So we diverge here. The `da.random.uniform` version correctly goes `(X, 0, 0), (y, 0), fit-0, ...` at this point.
The zarr version throws `zarr-X.zarr` on the stack and then starts working through `(X, 9, 0), (X, 8, 0), ... (y, 9), (y, 8), ... fit-0, fit-1, ..., fit-9)`

Your first question is really a C++ one: when you write ``new Object()``, actually two things happen: 

1. C++ calls ::operator new () to allocate memory
2. C++ calls the in-place constructor to construct the instance in the allocated memory region

pybind11 does 1, and the code in __init__ has to do 2. Otherwise you have an instance that believes it is constructed, but it just contains uninitialized memory.

I used this Python code regularly to create certificates for remote hosts that are not web servers and just discovered today that it has been removed. Considering that this is a command line utility for server configuration, I have to say I am puzzled by the concern about "visual noise" and "distraction." SSL certificates are widely used for all manner of services, but the current form of the command output and the thinking I have been able to cover that informs it points to an assumption that SSL is only for web servers. The instructions in the output take for a given that the user is making the certificate for a web server, and that an httpd is already running on the target host. For users who do not fit that mold, this is confusing.

If the aesthetic objections to having certbot --manual output this Python code are too strong to entertain returning it, I would at least like to ask that some provision be made for situations where the target host is not a web server. Asking people to install, configure, and run a permanent httpd as a prerequisite to having an SSL certificate on any given host seems inappropriately heavy-handed.

and we need `answers` to be of the form `[{"answer":["testA"]},{"answer":["testB"]}]`, this isn't possible with Lexicon current APIs. See https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/8b649338b6c03c7bfb2d465249cd759de62296cb/lexicon/providers/nsone.py#L35.

Sure, we could start implementing DNS provider APIs ourselves, but that kind of defeats the purpose of us using Lexicon.

I think that if we want to use GPU preprocessing in the data loader we would be restraining our users to use Python 2, which might be a bit too much.

Also, I'm not 100% convinced that in the setup that you showed it would be better to perform operations on the GPU.

The reason why I'm not convinced is that if we perform all the data augmentation on the CPU, then the GPU is free to run (asynchronously!) the network, while the different threads of the data loader will be loading and preprocessing data in the background.
If we have transforms in the GPU, then the data augmentation and the network will be competing for resources, making either the network run slower or the batches with the data not being ready when the network has finished the batch.

Did you have the chance to see if performing the operations on the GPU was actually useful in a training pipeline?

I'm not sure about MNIST, but LSUN has a validation set in its official dataset, while CIFAR does not. You can easily create a validation set in CIFAR using this code:

`class ChunkSampler(sampler.Sampler):
    """Samples elements sequentially from some offset. 
    Arguments:
        num_samples: # of desired datapoints
        start: offset where we should start selecting from
    """
    def __init__(self, num_samples, start = 0):
        self.num_samples = num_samples
        self.start = start

    def __iter__(self):
        return iter(range(self.start, self.start + self.num_samples))

    def __len__(self):
        return self.num_samples

NUM_TRAIN = 49000

Personally, I vote for having a default behavior that assumes a range [0, 1] and doesn't normalize the image.
Doing otherwise would result in issues like the mentioned #41 and potentially introduce bugs difficult to spot (as the normalization is "silent").

For the issue with different datatypes in different datasets, MNIST is a pickled object when downloaded from source, whereas SVHN is stored as a `.mat` file, and uses `scipy.io` to load it. `scipy` uses `numpy` and hence the returned value is of type `numpy.int64`.

Yes, maybe a python file will be easier to code and less error prone, but probably less obvious what is configuration and what is working script.
An example use-case I see behind such deserialization:  let's say there is a script `train.py` that takes a `train_config.yaml` as a single argument:
```bash
python train.py train_config.yaml
```
Such `train_config.yaml`  can be something like:
```
n_epochs: 100
batch_size: 32
...
model: 
   MySuperModel:
      n_classes: 12
      n_channels: 3      
....
train_transforms:
  Compose:
    transforms:
      - ToThreeBands:
      - ToPILImage:
      - RandomApply:
          p: 0.85
          transforms:
            - RandomAffine:
                degrees: [-45, 45]
                translate: [0.15, 0.15]
                scale: [1.0, 1.2]
            - RandomHorizontalFlip:
            - RandomVerticalFlip:
      - ToTensor:
      - _Normalize:
```
and the script `train.py` deserializes  all parameters, objects and assembles all together to run a training.

The same can be done with a python config file, where we can code directly a transformation as `torchvision` objects (but I'm less fan)

@mitar and I just looked into this a little more.

Interestingly, if you modify

https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/f4f3478b45ac047bc354470244f065f0889c4a48/python/ray/worker.py#L691-L692

to add 

```python
pickle.loads(pickled_function)
```

right after `pickle.dumps`, then it fails right there (because `pickled_function` is `b'\x80\x04'`). So the error is happening with the call to `pickle.dumps`.

If we try it in a loop, e.g.,

```python
while True:
    pickled_function = pickle.dumps(function)
    try:
        pickle.loads(pickled_function)
    except:
        print("Failed to load")
    else:
        break
```

then that seems to "fix" the issue. The call to `pickle.loads` may fail on the first try but will work in subsequent passes through the loop.

It seems like the issue must be related to some threading or something weird.

But after discussing with @gabisurita I think she's right here, `TypeConversionDispatcher` is not a declarative class, it contains implementation details that I'm not sure we want to expose as public api. I think that enabling the user to convert new cornice types hiding the implementation details is the way to go here

You can argue that we can do it in a imperative way here, something like
```python
swagger = CorniceSwagger()
swagger.add_type_converter(MyType, MyTypeConverter)
```
and I'm keen on this.

+1 on refactoring the interface of the `TypeConversionDispatcher`, I don't like the __call__ either. To be completely honest, I also don't like that the the swagger document is produced on `CorniceSwagger.__call__`, I mean, I can't see a valid reason for the `CorniceSwagger` instance to be a callable.

Well I don't know where to put this so I guess I'll just leave it here. Below is the link to the script and the description. If this is a problem, let me know.

https://www.dropbox.com/s/048p1u5wheo1r9d/livestreamlinkgui-public

This is LiveStreamLinkGUI. It's a lightweight GUI for Livestreamer, Streamlink, and any future forks. I made it for the personal use of my family and myself, but when I looked around, I saw people making requests for things I've already implemented. So I decided to share it. Its only requirements are BASH, rtmpdump, and zenity. I wrote it in Linux, for Linux, but because it's a BASH script, it should (in theory) work for any platform that supports BASH and its requirements. I hear Win10 has some kind of BASH support but I have no idea how capable it is of running this or any other script.


Features:
Open Chats.
Some websites have popout chats, such as twitch and vaughnlive. When you give LiveStreamLinkGUI a link for one of these sites, it can ask if you want to open the chat for that stream in your browser. It's important to know only the chat will be opened. So you don't have to worry about your browser eating up a lot of your system resources just to take part in the chat. I designed LiveStreamLinkGUI in a way where adding support for other sites shouldn't be difficult at all. All you need to know is the popout chat's URL and how to parse it.

Save Links.
LiveStreamLinkGUI can also save links. This makes it easy to keep track of your favorite streams and even open them without running your browser.

Loop Forever.
If the stream is lost or closed for whatever reason, you have the option to "loop forever". While doing so, LiveStreamLinkGUI will keep trying to reopen the stream until the script is manually killed. This is best used in a media setup, so you can keep watching your streams while you fall asleep, work, clean the house, or whatever. It works very well with the shutdown command. An example of the shutdown command (which can differ from distro to distro) is sudo shutdown -hP <minutes>, which will shutdown the system after the specified number of minutes. So it's similar to sleepmode for TVs.

I've added support for sites like arconaitv.me, funhaus.roosterteeth.com, and a few other sites. LiveStreamLinkGUI is designed so that adding support for other sites shouldn't be much of a chore.


Configurations:
All of the user specific configurations are at the top of the script. I would recommend going through them before running the script, but there is also a first time setup GUI which will help take you through the more basic configurations.


How to use:
If you want to open a new stream, all you have to do is execute the script and select "Open A New Link". A text input box will appear and you can drag and drop the link to that text box and click "OK". You can also copy-paste the link or manually type it in, if you so choose.

When the player closes, you're presented with a list of options. You can reopen it (if the stream is online), save the link for future use, loop it forever, open a new stream, open a saved stream, etc, etc. To save you a click, selecting "Close Program", is the same as clicking "Cancel", pressing ESC, or closing the window in any other way. So you don't have to scroll to the bottom every time you're done with the script.

For ease of use, I have a launcher for LiveStreamLinkGUI in my DE's panel. For media machines, I also keep a launcher for timed shutdowns. It makes it very easy to watch streams while falling asleep using only a pointer device. I'm not telling you what to do, I'm just telling you what I do.


As a side note, there's a VLC script that allows VLC to play Youtube playlists. Naturally, LiveStreamLinkGUI gets along very well with this.


Feel free to make whatever changes you want with this. If you make a change, such as adding chat support for other sites, or site support for any sites livestreamer/streamlink don't support, please let me know so I can add the changes too. If you redistribute this script, I would like to ask that you please give me credit as "Mouse". Thank you and enjoy!

In this stream, there are links to the individual qualities.  While the `resolution` part of every other line does not really mean much as it does not need to be accurate, or even exist, it is good for this example.

In this example, there are 4 different 720p streams.  Streamlink, by default, only cares about 3 of these.  

```
Available streams: 180p_alt, 360p_alt, 360p_alt2, 540p_alt, 720p_alt, 720p_alt2, 180p (worst), 360p, 540p, 720p (best)
```

The last line should be the `720p (best)` stream.  

The `720p_alt` stream should be the second to last link, and also has a resolution of `1280x720` and a bandwidth of `2264000`, therefore it can be considered a mirror.  

The `720p_alt2` stream should be the second to last link, and while it has a resolution of `1280x720`, it does have a bandwidth of `1864000`, therefore, it would be a slightly lower quality.  

---

A great example of the need for the `alt` streams comes from The Weather Channel stream. the image build from the host environment (this is useful if you don't want

only visible during the build - it should not be propagated to the
container at runtime, although, I don't really care too much if it is (if
there is no other way). My understanding is that they could be kept out of
the singularity image's execution environment by using the %post section:
http://singularity.lbl.gov/docs-recipes#environment

@tacaswell If you're interested in Unix specifically, and not sockets, then I think `trio.hazmat.wait_readable`/`trio.hazmat.wait_writable` should give you what you need – the awkwardness that this issue is about is specifically a side-effect of dealing with sockets across Windows/Unix. (Also now I'm curious what you're working on but probably there's a better place to talk about that then this issue :-).)

I *really* doubt it's Python specific; the Python socket module is a pretty thin wrapper.

Do keep in mind that localhost networking is Often Weird – this may or may not happen or cause a problem on real networks. And unless you're actually planning to have clients receive slowly for some reason, that part may not be representative either – in theory the OS is trying to adapt the receive buffer to match the characteristics of the network, which here is very fast, not the application. And eventually we'll have stuff like BBR that makes TCP do the right thing even if the buffers are the wrong size.

But yeah for now there's a limit to what you can accomplish purely through TCP backpressure... It's getting better but...

Traffic shaping isn't really something that works on localhost; it's about making sure you don't send packets faster than your known outgoing bandwidth. `TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT` is also about throttling outgoing data; it can't help if the receive side has decided to buffer up a bunch of data.

I agree that sysctl is probably the wrong solution :-). Is this causing an measurable end-to-end problem?

Another general strategy (hack) is to do your own simple flow control, like: "every time the client receives a message, it sends back a message saying 'thanks, got it', and the server waits until it sees that before sending the next message". As written this will kill your throughput, but it sounds like for this application you might not care, and the resulting latency is within a factor of two of optimal.

If we do do something like this, then I think it'll be by exposing some function in hazmat that lets you declare that a task should be considered "not blocked" even if it looks like it is (and also a way to set it back afterwards of course). Then `run_sync_in_worker_thread` would use the API, instead of being special cased.

Ideally, worker threads should only be used for blocking (io bound) code. If you try to use them for CPU bound code, then you run into bugs in CPython's GIL, where it ends up starving one of the threads. So maybe the `getaddrinfo` case is the typical one, and it's fair to treat calls to `run_sync_in_worker_thread` as blocking, on the assumption that the code in the thread will be blocking.

On the other hand, when using autojump_clock, there's something weird about the idea that part of your program just jumped forward in time, but the getaddrinfo didn't get the memo and is still running. It is a general limitation of autojump_clock that it only works on stuff actually running inside trio; it can't control threads, or that memcached server you spun up to run your tests against, etc. And when trio is running something in a worker thread, it means trio *knows* that it can't pull its magic trick and make it seamless, so maybe it shouldn't try.

The SSL branch (#107) has a set of ABCs for streams that I'm pretty happy with. One place that I realized it's lacking, though, while trying to write tests for SSL-over-streams, is in specifying exception handling. In particular, there are some pretty generic things that can happen to streams:

- `recv` but the other end has closed the connection: stdlib sockets return `b""`
- `sendall` but the other end has closed the connection: stdlib sockets on Linux & MacOS raise `BrokenPipeError` (which is a bit confusing, since there are no pipes involved, but, you know, Unix), and on Windows raise `ConnectionAbortedError:  [WinError 10053] An established connection was aborted by the software in your host machine`
- `recv` but the connection has already been closed (i.e., locally someone called `close` and resources have been freed): stdlib sockets on Linux & MacOS raise `OSError: [Errno 9] Bad file descriptor`, and on Windows raise `OSError: [WinError 10038] An operation was attempted on something that is not a socket`
- `sendall` but the connection has already been closed (in the same sense): stdlib sockets on Linux & MacOS raise `OSError: [Errno 9] Bad file descriptor`, and on Windows raise `OSError: [WinError 10038] An operation was attempted on something that is not a socket`

(Notes: The last two cases are due to python sockets setting their internal fileno to -1 when closing. In the second case, the WinError 10053 is [WSAECONNABORTED](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms740668(v=vs.85).aspx).)

There's also ECONNRESET / `ConnectionResetError` which can happen when the remote side has closed the connection, from both `recv` and `sendall`, depending apparently on whether there's unACKed data sitting in the TCP send buffer ([some details](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/2974021/what-does-econnreset-mean-in-the-context-of-an-af-local-socket)). And WSAENETRESET, and a few other WSA things that might or might not be related. Twisted does `from errno import WSAECONNRESET as ECONNABORTED`, which is odd given that WSAECONNABORTED exists and is something else, but seems to be specifically for `accept`.

And SSL wrappers add their own exciting ways of wanting to report these same failure modes. I'm guessing when we get to subprocesses then pipes/named pipes will have their own weirdness.

So there's at the very least a temptation to collapse these down into:

* `recv` but the connection is gone (somehow, network broken, whatever): return `b""`
* `sendall` but the connection is gone (somehow, network broken, whatever): raise `BrokenStream`
* `recv` after local close: raise `ClosedStream`
* `sendall` after local close: raise `ClosedStream`

And of course we'd use exception chaining to preserve the original cause as well, so at least for debugging the information is there. The question is whether this would be making it hard to reliably/programmatically access information that turns out to be important, like if for some reason an application calling `sendall` needs to respond differently depending on whether that gives `ECONNABORTED` vs `ECONNRESET`.

TCP [distinguishes between FIN and RST](https://www.quora.com/What-is-the-difference-between-TCPs-FIN-and-RST-packets) as ways of ending a connection. IIUC the difference is that FIN is a regular "eof" packet (e.g. sent by `shutdown(SHUT_WR)`) and gets ACKed; the orderly way to shut down a connection is for both sides to send FIN and then ACK them. OTOH RST just means "no go away" and is sent when e.g. unexpected data arrives; it doesn't get ACKed. When we call `close` I'm not entirely sure what happens – maybe sending a FIN plus if any new packets arrive sending a RST?

Maybe the thing to do is to allow `recv` to raise `BrokenStreamError`, so `recv() == b""` indicates FIN, and everything else is an error? (Oh also I decided between sentences that probably these exceptions should have `Error` in the name.)

Actually.... this *doesn't* need help from Trio, if we're clever. It's not the most elegant thing, but we can start a timer thread running, and then if the timeout expires it can call back into Trio. Of course we'll also want to be able to kill it early, so something like, a thread blocked in `select` would work.

```python
def timeout_thread(wake_sock, timeout, trio_token, cancel_scope):
    readable, _, _ = select.select([wake_sock], [], [], timeout)
    if not readable:
        # we were woken by the timeout expiring
        trio_token.run_sync_soon(cancel_scope.cancel)

async with trio.open_nursery() as nursery:
    a, b = socket.socketpair()  # blocking sockets
    trio_token = trio.hazmat.current_trio_token()
    try:
        with trio.open_cancel_scope() as timeout_cancel_scope:
            nursery.start_soon(trio.run_sync_in_worker_thread, timeout_thread, b, timeout, trio_token, timeout_cancel_scope, limiter=UNLIMITED)
            # ... do actual test here ...
    finally:
        a.send(b"x")
        if timeout_cancel_scope.cancelled_caught:
            raise ...
```

This does require that the Trio scheduler be functioning properly, but so would a more intrusive version baked into the Trio scheduler. This also has the advantage that by being in a third-party library we have a lot of flexibility to adjust the response to the timeout however we want – e.g. instead of just cancelling the test, we could have it capture and print a snapshot of the task tree with stacktraces. Or try cancelling after X seconds, and then if the test is still running after another Y seconds (i.e. it's ignoring the cancellation), use harsher methods to disable pytest's output capturing, dump debugging information to the screen, and then call `os._exit()` to crash the process.

@ebarsoum This could be very confusing for the Tensorflow world as 'VALID' = no padding as opposed to no **auto** padding. On the other hand, we will try to put aside my presumptions from Tensorflow and explain the scheme that I think is the most intuitive is:

You specify either autopad or pads. When both are specified, we should have a precedence established from discussion and take the one with higher precedence and abandon the one with lower precedence. Your current specification has the following drawbacks:

1. The interaction of auto_pad and pads are asymmetrical. Meaning that in some cases you go down to pads after looking at auto_pad whereas in some other cases you dont. Such asymmetrical behavior will lead to user having a hard time to memorize your specification and results in time wasted in looking up documentation and debugging.

2. I do not see why anyone would specify audo_pad to be 'VALID' and then specify pads to be non-empty. Can you give me a specific use case? Why use 'VALID' in any case if you can just leave pads empty?

3. On a conceptually level, VALID is an automatic padding scheme (as manifested by its naming). You seem to take it as a partial padding scheme. VALID option currently has two functions:
- specify no padding is needed. (in which case no information is gained by having this 'VALID' paramter since we can leave pads empty).
- specify users' intent that padding should be done according to the pads attribute (again, no information is gained by having this 'VALID' parameter since it is overiden).
- So my question is why would we have this 'VALID' option?

However, it's super hard to express the numpy broadcast logic for dynamic shapes (because that means we can only use Operators to express it). Besides, this will help backends to adopt out new braodcast semantics because they can split a broadcast-add to first do materialize broadcast (aka. expand here) and then do non-broadcast-add.

My bad, I should have motivated this better (and also, perhaps FeatureDropout is not a very good name).

"FeatureDropout", also known as "Dropout2d/Dropout3d" in PyTorch (http://pytorch.org/docs/master/nn.html#torch.nn.Dropout2d) slightly modifies the Dropout algorithm so that only channels (the first two dimensions as in NCHW ordering; the important thing is that it matches the output of Conv) are randomly masked out; the actual pixels aren't randomly masked (since the correlations between adjacent pixels means that the masking is just decreasing the effective learning rate.)

It's a fairly important technique and shows up in some models that we are interested in exporting.

We need all args of the class, otherwise, we can't fully store the behaviour of the layer, then we can't reconstruct the network from the graph.

For example:
- For `DenseLayer`, we need act, n_units.
- For `Conv2d`, we need act, n_filter, filter_size, stride, padding (alternatively, we can get the n_filter, filter_size from the shape of `W_conv2d`, and get the `stride` by comparing the outputs shape the this layer and previous layer.)

There is still a problem with the current implementation of stacked decorators.

Let's consider we have two different validators, `v1` and `v2`, and we want to apply them to the same function, but on different HTTP verbs:

```python
service = Service(name="service", path="/service")

@service.get(validator=v1)
@service.post(validator=v2)
def my_function(request):
    # do some stuff
    return "yay"
```

With the current implementation, both validators are checked, even if I'm just doing a GET on the /service. Same behaviour exists for content type validation.

I'm working on a patch for this.
